You may not want to hear this. Now, I don't know what market you're in, but is Facebook efficient for you, or is it effective? Effective. OK. Because in certain markets, the quality of customer you're getting from Facebook is significantly less in terms of lifetime customer value, then you could get in other media. In other media, you don't have that issue to deal with. I'm probably the most ambitiously lazy guy you can meet. I don't want a challenge. I have enough challenges. I've decided to learn jazz guitar improvisation. That and life are enough challenges for me. In business, I don't want a challenge. I just want to get from point A to point B. This being the money, in media plays a big part in that. So if there's a low-hanging fruit way to get it, and I know this is not the answer you want, I'm going for the low-hanging fruit way, which in most cases, for me is not Facebook. What market are you in? And look, I'll just piggyback something else you won't want to hear. How ever effective and profitable it is, depending on it, is very dangerous. Extremely. Because the problem that you've just described is only going to get worse. They want brand advertisers who don't count, who don't do math. That's what they want. And they are increasingly getting them. And as soon as they have enough of them, they will make it harder and harder and harder for direct response people to be there at all. And that's really the history of media. So relying on it and not building out your plan B, just solving the problem of plan A, is a really bad business strategy. I mean, it's really bad. Who was sext? Thanks for everything so far. You alluded earlier on that we're all worrying far too much about the features and the benefits. So how do you establish what the real market drivers are in your business? And are there three tips to finding out what those market drivers truly are? And are there any resources that we can use to help us establish that? A couple good resources are a good old Amazon getting on there and reading customer testimonials for a similar product. I'll be honest with you. That's not my first preference. That's not my first go-to thing. I'm old school. I would really prefer to get face-to-face belly-to-belly with the market. Again, you know what? I think it's probably somewhat of a misnomer to talk about markets. We're talking about people. And the closer you can get to understanding what the people want and the closer you can get to understanding your avatar, the better. The best way to do that is face-to-face belly-to-belly with somebody who buys what it is you sell, if that means hanging out at Walmart and talking to those people. If you can do that, do that. You're going to get a much more visceral impact and probably a much better understanding of the thought process. And you'll probably get a better understanding of the unspoken things that they really want, the real emotional needs that are trying to satisfy that way. So an OK substitute for that would be finding where those people congregate online and read what the writing, if there's a discussion form or Amazon testimonials. See, there's a ton of market research that can be useful, paid research. And so there's a couple of companies. I mean, if you Google market research and you work backwards, there's companies, for example, that compile the annual thing about the woman consumer and the annual thing about the senior consumer. And it's like this thick. And all these studies that have been done, I bought it all when I did the marketing to the Influent Book, for example. And Guthrie Ranker buys the women's stuff every year, so I see it. And it's not that there's not gems in there, but it is data. So it is largely statistical, demographic, psychographic. It's like polling in politics. So 22,000 people have been surveyed. And 42% of them rank this as their number one concern. And 36% of them rank this as their number one concern. If you get five of them drunk and have the same conversation with them, you'll have a totally different sense of who the hell they are than, you know, it's like I say at GKIC events all the time, I'm a military complain that people thought she had some kind of urinary tract infection or something, because I'm constantly pushing Uber into the women's room, go in there, hang out for a little while, come back and tell me what they're all saying, right? Because the truth will come out in a lady's room. That's where the truth will come out. So nothing really beats whoever it is you are going to sell to getting in amongst them and engaging in and listening to conversations and observing behavior. In a sense, the market research is like watching animals in a zoo versus watching them in the wild in their natural habitat left to their own devices. And it's not that watching them in the zoo is not useful, but it only gives us about half the picture. Last. Hi, Dan. Richard Dougal. Knowing human behavior, Dan, how are you able to keep such a high retention rate in your membership site among your nights? Ooh, that's a course within itself. So most people who have, again, we're going to talk about print. Most people who have membership sites that are $30 a month or more, you check with any of them, average retention two months. They get three months. They are thrilled. Now, damaging emission, I have not checked my retention stats so far for this year. When I checked last year, it was an average of 18 months at a $98 a month price point. Now, there are very specific reasons why. This was a carefully and meticulously engineered process which the best way to see is to join my nights in the market in Camelot and see what happens. You knew I had to get a sales pitch. Yeah, that's fine. But a big part of that retention is the print newsletter. Again, I'm going to repeat myself. It cannot, the emotional impact, the footprint in the human brain from that being delivered in print, cannot be replicated any other way with digital delivery, any other way. I will stop doing it when it stops getting spectacular results like this. But I don't think we're going to see that for a very long time. That's a big part of it, Richard. See, there's a reason. There's a reason it's called virtual. Right? So think of the difference between emailing a picture of flowers to your significant other or coming home with flowers. The whole dynamic is different, right? And yet, the minute that works, I'm all in. Neither he nor I, and people really believe it about me and they're wrong, I am agnostic on this entire topic. So the minute we don't have to send out DVDs, and we could just tell people to go online and watch the DVD, the minute I see sleep number beds do it, I'm all in. I'd love for all my clients to be able to do it. That's more money they can give me. If they got to send out 100,000 DVDs this year and I can eliminate that, I can make more money, right? So I'm agnostic on all this. I am not ideological. I am not Obama, where, you know, he will freely admit that if we triple capital gains taxes, it's going to have a negative effect on the economy, but I want to do it anyway because it's the right thing to do. That ain't me, right? I'm a pragmatist, as is he. So we would eliminate print tomorrow without an ounce of remorse. I would cheaply produce my VSLs with a camera head mounted with a smart phone duct taped to a pole. If I could get away with it, I'd just charge more now to write the damn thing, because it is what I make him spend, you know, 100,000 to produce the videos. I'm limited in how much I can charge. So I'm agnostic on this, and he is agnostic on this. But he's, but again, think about it, email or flowers or bring flowers out. All of the emotional embedded dynamics are different. Their retention rate will go up if they also deliver the extra goodies in hard version. And some are willing to do that and some aren't willing to do that. And even better, if the GLOP is delivered on one day, every month the extra goodies delivered on another day. You got it. It doesn't double the cost, but it increases the cost maybe by half, right. It increases retention by a whole lot more, right.
